answer:
**The work is *Gaṇapatitattva* (slug: `unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136`), and the rite appears at the close of Chapter 1.**[^1]

This chapter ends with a *paṅlukatan* (purification ritual) for Gaṇapati (a son of Śiva), usable for exorcism. It specifies an *ampel gading* (ivory bamboo) bearing the *rajah* (magical drawing) of the deity—Gaṇa—with a discus (*cakra*) in his left hand and a mace (*gadā*) in his right—along with a white-and-yellow *ajuman* set, a *sucī* offering whose main dish is a white crested chicken (*ayam blĕngong putih*), water in a *sanku* (copper vessel), *sudhāmala* flowers with *pras* and *santun* offerings, a monetary offering of eleven hundred, and *samsam* of *katima* leaves.[^2]

Its protections work against an epidemic, epilepsy, spirit affliction, and destruction caused by animals.[^3]

The deity's invocation hails him thus:

> Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga and Dṛṅgī (?),  
> who makes opposing diseases faint, who gives life to the three worlds. // (58)[^4]

[^1]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1, [Chapter 1](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/).

[^2]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1, [p-36c962](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-36c962).

[^3]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1, [p-0dbd7d](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-0dbd7d).

[^4]: *Gaṇapatitattva*, Chapter 1, [p-a89bcc](/works/unknown-ganapatitattva-66a136/01/translation/#p-a89bcc).

args.query: Destroyer of all poisons, Lord of Kāladṛṅga
